content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

Contact: JOHN KEPLINGER                          FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE
916/322-5662                                     Wednesday, Jan. 11


                                                 m1-98-347


LEGISLATORS MAY ATTEND LOBBYISTS' LUNCHEON


The Fair Political Practices Commission ruled today that members

of a lobbyist organization may entertain legislators at weekly luncheons

provided there is no behind-the-scenes arrangement between lobbyists to

wine and dine lawmakers.

The Commission made it clear, however, that if any evidence

develops to indicate the existence of an explicit or implicit agreement

or understanding among the lobbyists to violate the law's $10 per month

spending limitation it will crack down on the luncheons.

California's Political Reform Act, which the Commission admin-

isters, prohibits lobbyists from spending more than $10 a month to

entertain individual legislators or other state officials.

The Commission's decision was issued in an opinion requested

by Merle J. Goddard, president of the Institute of Governmental

Advocates, an organization of approximately 50 lobbyists which holds

weekly luncheons when the Legislature is in session.

The organization asked the Commission if members could invite

legislators as guests to the luncheons and pay for their food and

beverage if the attendance of such officials occurred on a random and

infrequent basis.

(more)

Legislators May Attend Lobbyists' Luncheon--2

The Commission approved, but only if the lobbyist members acted

independently and did not arrange among themselves which legislators

would be invited.

"Although the instant situation presents a close case, we

conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and

the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate

the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members

to make gifts to an official totaling more than $10 in a month," the

Commission's opinion said.

#

1-78

sections.conclusion: Although the instant situation presents a close case, we conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members to make gifts to an official totaling more than $10 in a month.
sections.question_synthetic: May members of a lobbyist organization entertain legislators at weekly luncheons and pay for their meals, and if so, under what conditions does this comply with the $10 per month spending limitation on gifts to state officials?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC approved lobbyist members entertaining legislators at weekly luncheons provided that attendance is random and infrequent, members act independently without arrangement regarding which legislators are invited, and there is no implicit agreement among lobbyists to circumvent the $10 monthly spending limit. The Commission warned it will take action if evidence later emerges of any illicit or implicit agreement to violate this limitation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Press release format rather than traditional advice letter; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections but contains clear legal analysis and ruling. Document dated January 11, 1978. Some text appears damaged/OCR errors (e.g., 'hegislators' instead of 'Legislators'). Contact person and phone number included for media inquiries.

QUESTION: May members of a lobbyist organization entertain legislators at weekly luncheons and pay for their meals, and if so, under what conditions does this comply with the $10 per month spending limitation on gifts to state officials?

CONCLUSION: Although the instant situation presents a close case, we conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members to make gifts to an official totaling more than $10 in a month.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Contact: JOHN KEPLINGER FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE 916/322-5662 Wednesday, Jan. 11 m1-98-347 LEGISLATORS MAY ATTEND LOBBYISTS' LUNCHEON The Fair Political Practices Commission ruled today that members of a lobbyist organization may entertain legislators at weekly luncheons provided there is no behind-the-scenes arrangement between lobbyists to wine and dine lawmakers. The Commission made it clear, however, that if any evidence develops to indicate the existence of an explicit or implicit agreement or understanding among the lobbyists to violate the law's $10 per month spending limitation it will crack down on the luncheons. California's Political Reform Act, which the Commission admin- isters, prohibits lobbyists from spending more than $10 a month to entertain individual legislators or other state officials. The Commission's decision was issued in an opinion requested by Merle J. Goddard, president of the Institute of Governmental Advocates, an organization of approximately 50 lobbyists which holds weekly luncheons when the Legislature is in session. The organization asked the Commission if members could invite legislators as guests to the luncheons and pay for their food and beverage if the attendance of such officials occurred on a random and infrequent basis. (more) Legislators May Attend Lobbyists' Luncheon--2 The Commission approved, but only if the lobbyist members acted independently and did not arrange among themselves which legislators would be invited. "Although the instant situation presents a close case, we conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members to make gifts to an official totaling more than $10 in a month," the Commission's opinion said. # 1-78
embedding.summary: The FPPC issued an opinion approving the Institute of Governmental Advocates' practice of allowing member lobbyists to entertain legislators at weekly luncheons and pay for meals, provided attendance is random and members do not coordinate invitations. The Commission emphasized strict enforcement if evidence emerges of any coordinated arrangement to exceed the $10 per month gift limitation.
